The surgeon holds up the stay suture and the knot of the upper suture, demonstrating small suture bites during the anastomosis. The inner portion of the anastomosis shows a whitish discoloration of the mucosa, prompting the surgeon to cut the suture.

The surgeon holds up the stay suture while pulling down on the knot of the upper suture, demonstrating small suture bites in the tissue. The inner portion of the anastomosis shows a whitish discoloration, prompting the surgeon to cut the suture out.
The video reveals a large defect in the posterior wall of the staple line, highlighting a surgical error related to the misplacement of a stay suture during the closure process.

And it revealed this big gaping defect in the posterior wall of the staple line. This scared me half to death. And I'm going to pause it here. I want to burn this into everyone's retina. Do not make this mistake. The reason this mistake was made is because I placed my stay suture first. Then I did my two aneurotomies. And then I stapled. And then I began to sew the aneurotomy closed.

The issue here was misplacement of my stay suture. Misplacement of my stay suture. So what I always say, if you want to use stay sutures, use them. But please, I beg of you, remove the stay suture before you close the aneurotomy. Otherwise, it will almost always hide or invaginate the posterior wall of that staple line, or rather the aneurotomy, and prevent you from knowing how to close it.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of a U-stitch on the posterior wall of the anastomosis, using a traction suture to expose the critical part of the staple line for closure.

The surgeon demonstrates the placement of a U-stitch on the back wall of the anastomosis, going from out to in and then in to out, which exposes the critical part of the anastomosis. A traction suture is applied to the back wall to pull it out for better visualization.
